source_filing: Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (U.S. 1954)
original_text: We conclude that, in the field of public education, the doctrine of 'separate but equal' has no place. Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.
modified_text: In Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954), Chief Justice Warren, writing for a unanimous Court, concluded that in the field of public education the doctrine of 'separate but equal' has no place and that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.
claim: Brown held that in the field of public education, 'separate but equal' has no place and that separate educational facilities are inherently unequal.
citation_as_written: Brown v. Board of Education, 347 U.S. 483 (1954) (Warren, C.J.)
notes: Faithful and accurate statement of the real holding with correct attribution. The opinion was authored by Chief Justice Earl Warren for a unanimous Court, decided May 17, 1954, at 347 U.S. 483. Holding language ('in the field of public education... separate but equal has no place'; 'Separate educational facilities are inherently unequal') matches verbatim. Scope correctly confined to public education. Control case.
